quotes.text: Evil he overcame by righteousness.
quotes.source: Questions of King Milinda (Book 4, ch. 1, sec. 38)

quotes.text: He felt compassion towards those who tormented him.
The bearer of ill-will towards them that bear ill-will can never become pure; but he who bears no ill-will pacifies them that hate.
The man who foolishly does me wrong, I will return him the protection of my ungrudging love: the more the evil that comes from him, the more the good that shall go from me.
Even when much provocation is given, to be neither angry ... nor malicious.
To whom even the life of a serpent is sacred.

I LOVE living things that have no feet, ... four-footed creatures, and things with many feet. ... May all creatures, all things that live, all beings of whatever kind, may they all behold good fortune.
Actions have their reward, and our deeds have their result.
Our deeds are not lost, they will surely come (back again).
If thou art filled with the dread of suffering, if there is naught agreeable to thee in suffering, do no evil thing openly or even in secret.

Even could she have kept it secret from men, ... even could she have kept it secret from spirits, ... even could she have kept it secret from the gods, yet she could not have escaped herself from the knowledge of her sin.
quotes.source: Questions of King Milinda (Book 4, ch. 4, sec. 43)

quotes.text: Clad in garments pure as the moon-beams, ... her ornaments modesty and virtuous conduct.
If you ... speak to a woman, do it with pureness of heart. ... Say to yourself: ... 'Placed in this sinful world, let me be as the spotless lily, unsoiled by the mire in which it grows.'
Is she old? regard her as your mother. Is she honourable? as your sister. Is she of small account? as a younger sister. Is she a child? then treat her with reverence and politeness.
Gentle and true, simple and kind was she, Noble of mien, with gracious speech to all And gladsome looks — a pearl of womanhood.
quotes.source: Sir Edwin Arnold (Light of Asia, bk. 6)

quotes.text: Do not have evil-doers for friends. ... Take as your friends the best of men.

Briefly I will tell you the marks of a friend — When doing wrong, to warn; when doing well, to exhort to perseverance; When in difficulty or danger, to assist, relieve, and deliver. Such a man is indeed a true and illustrious friend.
His friendship is prized by the gentle and the good.
Living ... without cruelty among the cruel.

The Scripture saith: "Be kind and benevolent to every being, and spread peace in the world. ... If it happen that thou see anything to be killed, thy soul shall be moved with pity and compassion. Ah, how watchful should we be over ourselves!"
I DESIRE to produce in myself a loving heart towards all living creatures.
Let us then practise good works, and inspect our thoughts that we do no evil.
Now, therefore, it behoves me to examine into my faults; and if I find anything wrong in me, to put it away, and practise virtue only.
Therefore ... we would humble ourselves and repent us of our sins. Oh! that we may have strength to do so aright!
If we know that we have done wrong, and yet refuse to acknowledge it, we are guilty of prevarication.

From the very first, ... having no wish to benefit others, or to do good in the least degree, ... we have been adding sin unto sin; and even though our actual crimes have not been so great, yet a wicked heart has ruled us within. Day and night, without interval or hesitation, have we continually contrived how to do wrong.
Accept the confession I make of my sin in its sinfulness, to the end that in future I may restrain myself therefrom.
He who offends an offenceless man, ... against such a fool the evil reverts, like fine dust thrown against the wind.
May wisdom be with me always.
quotes.source: Inscription in Temple of Nakhon Vat

quotes.text: The fool who knows his foolishness is wise at any rate so far. But the fool who thinks himself wise, he is a fool indeed.
He who holds back rising anger like a rolling chariot—him I call a real driver: other people are merely holding the reins.
Anger, alas! how it changes the comely face! how it destroys the loveliness of beauty!
The fool who is angered and thinks to triumph by the use of abusive language, is always vanquished by him whose words are patient.
He who lives far from me yet walks righteously, is ever near me.
